cards.title: The Extension System Overview
cards.teaser: DuckDB ships lean and grows on demand — extensions add capabilities without bloating the core binary or violating the zero-dependency philosophy.

runs.text: DuckDB's core library has no external dependencies and a narrow feature surface by design. Extensions fill in everything beyond basic SQL: cloud storage access, geographic queries, full-text search, file format readers, and more. You load only what your workload needs.

items.text: When you run a query that requires an extension (e.g.,
), DuckDB can automatically install and load the required extension if
downloads the extension binary from the repository.
activates it in the current session.
Installed extensions persist across sessions. Loaded extensions must be re-loaded each session unless you configure autoload.
Distributed by the DuckDB team from
Third-party, hosted in the community registry. Require explicit trust acknowledgment before install.

runs.text: Starting with DuckDB 1.1, autoload is enabled by default for known official extensions. A query that needs
will trigger install automatically in interactive sessions. In production scripts, prefer explicit
Like a language's standard library vs its package manager — the core ships with essentials, and the registry provides the rest without requiring you to vendor everything upfront.

cards.title: The httpfs Extension
cards.teaser: httpfs is the foundation for all remote data access in DuckDB — it adds HTTP, S3, GCS, and Azure Blob support to any file-reading function.

runs.text: extension makes DuckDB's file-reading functions location-agnostic. Once loaded, functions like
accept remote URLs in addition to local paths.

items.text: — AWS S3 (and S3-compatible stores like MinIO, Cloudflare R2)
— Hugging Face dataset hub (added in DuckDB 1.1)

runs.text: httpfs streams data rather than downloading files in full before querying. A Parquet file with row group metadata on S3 allows DuckDB to fetch only the row groups that satisfy a filter predicate — significantly reducing egress costs on large datasets.
Like a filesystem driver that teaches your application to treat S3 URLs the same way it treats local file paths — you get the same API, different storage backend.

cards.title: The Iceberg Extension
cards.teaser: DuckDB reads Apache Iceberg tables natively as of 1.1 — no Spark, no JVM, just a SQL scan over a catalog-registered or path-based Iceberg table.

runs.text: Apache Iceberg is an open table format that adds schema evolution, time travel, partition pruning, and ACID transactions on top of Parquet files. DuckDB's

items.text: Point at the table's metadata directory on local disk or S3.
Connect to a Polaris, Nessie, or AWS Glue catalog via the Iceberg REST Catalog spec.
Query a table as it existed at a specific snapshot ID or timestamp.
The query planner respects Iceberg partition specs to avoid scanning unnecessary files.
Write support (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE into Iceberg tables) is not yet available as of early 2026 — reads only.
V1 and V2 table specs are supported; V3 (in-progress at the Iceberg project) is partial.

runs.text: For production Iceberg reads, always pin your DuckDB version. Iceberg's metadata parsing improvements between DuckDB patch versions can change query plans and performance characteristics in ways that matter for large tables.
Like a database that can mount an NFS share directly — you do not need to import the data; you query it in place through a standard protocol.

cards.title: The Delta Extension
cards.teaser: DuckDB reads Delta Lake tables via the delta extension, using the official delta-kernel-rs library — the same Rust kernel that powers Spark's Delta reader.

runs.text: Delta Lake is the other dominant open table format alongside Iceberg. DuckDB's
, the official Rust-based Delta Lake kernel contributed by Databricks, ensuring compatibility with Delta protocol versions written by Spark, Databricks, and other engines.

items.text: Supports deletion vectors, column mapping, and V3 table features available in Databricks Unity Catalog tables.
Respects Delta partition columns to skip files.
Reading Delta's Change Data Feed (CDF) to extract row-level change history.
Handles tables where columns have been added or dropped over time.
Write operations — reads only as of early 2026.
Deletion vector application on very old Delta tables written with protocol V1 only.

runs.text: If your data team uses Databricks and you need to query their tables from a Python pipeline without a Spark dependency,
over S3 is the practical solution. The delta-kernel-rs backing means protocol compatibility is maintained by the same team that writes the Delta spec.
Like a read-only mount of a filesystem format your OS did not natively support — a compatible driver lets you access the files without converting them.

cards.title: The Spatial Extension
cards.teaser: The spatial extension adds geometry types, dozens of ST_ functions, and the ability to read GeoJSON, Shapefiles, and GeoParquet directly into SQL queries.

runs.text: extension brings geographic and GIS capabilities to DuckDB. It wraps GDAL (for format reading) and GEOS/PROJ (for geometry operations) and exposes them through a standard SQL geometry type and
function API compatible with PostGIS conventions.

For city-scale spatial analysis — joining user locations to neighborhood polygons, computing drive-time buffers, aggregating event counts by region — the spatial extension handles workloads that would otherwise require PostGIS or a dedicated GIS platform.
Like PostGIS but as a library — the same ST_ function vocabulary you already know, loaded on demand without running a PostgreSQL server.

cards.title: The Full-Text Search Extension
cards.teaser: The FTS extension adds inverted index-based full-text search to DuckDB tables — useful for document search on datasets too large to scan with LIKE but too small to justify Elasticsearch.

runs.text: (full-text search) extension provides BM25-ranked inverted index search for text columns. It sits in the gap between
(no index, full scan) and a dedicated search engine like Elasticsearch (infrastructure overhead).

items.text: builds an inverted index stored as auxiliary DuckDB tables alongside the original.
function scores documents against a query using BM25 relevance ranking.
Indexes must be rebuilt manually after INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE — there is no incremental update.
No support for fuzzy matching, stemming (English only), or phonetic similarity.
Index rebuild is required after any data change — not suitable for high-write tables.
Not competitive with dedicated search engines for very large text corpora (millions of documents, complex query syntax).

runs.text: FTS indexes are stored as plain DuckDB tables. They are not transactionally linked to the source table — if you insert new rows without rebuilding the index, new documents are invisible to search. Always rebuild after bulk loads.
Like adding a search index to a SQLite table — useful for the "small dataset, no infrastructure" tier, with the same caveats about manual rebuild that SQLite's FTS5 has.

cards.title: The Excel Extension
cards.teaser: The Excel extension adds `read_xlsx()`, letting you query `.xlsx` files directly with SQL — no conversion step, no pandas.read_excel() intermediary.

runs.text: in some older documentation — they share a binary) provides
for reading Microsoft Excel files directly into DuckDB queries.

items.text: String, integer, float, date, datetime, and boolean cell types.
Merged cell detection (merged cells return the value in the top-left cell; other cells in the range are NULL).
Password-protected files — not supported; will error.

runs.text: Common use case — finance and ops teams often deliver data in
files. Instead of writing a conversion script, query the file directly:

explanation.code:
-- Join an Excel report against a database table
SELECT e.region, e.target, SUM(o.revenue) AS actual
FROM read_xlsx('targets.xlsx', header = true) e
JOIN orders o ON e.region = o.region
GROUP BY e.region, e.target;

runs.text: reads the entire sheet into memory before returning results — it does not stream. For very large Excel files (100K+ rows), exporting to CSV and using
will be faster and use less memory.
Like a file format driver — teaching your SQL engine to open

cards.title: The JSON Extension
cards.teaser: The JSON extension promotes JSON from a string column to a first-class queryable type — path extraction, nested array unnesting, and auto-schema inference all included.

runs.text: DuckDB's core includes basic JSON support, but the
extension (bundled as a core extension in 1.1+, so often pre-loaded) adds the full JSON path query API and format readers.

items.text: — extract a value at a JSONPath; returns JSON type.
— keys of a JSON object as a VARCHAR[].
— returns true/false; useful for data quality checks.
samples the file and infers column types — equivalent to Parquet's schema inference.
Nested objects become STRUCT columns; arrays become LIST columns.

runs.text: For JSON columns stored in a Parquet file or a DuckDB table,

operators are usually fast enough for exploratory queries. For production pipelines that repeatedly extract the same nested field, materialize it into a dedicated column at ingest time — repeated JSON path evaluation on large tables adds up.
Like jq embedded in SQL — the path query syntax is similar, but the result plugs directly into JOINs, GROUP BYs, and the rest of the query planner.

cards.title: The AWS Extension
cards.teaser: The AWS extension wires DuckDB into the AWS credential chain — profiles, IAM roles, and SSO tokens all work without hardcoding keys.

runs.text: extension plugs the AWS SDK credential provider chain into DuckDB's S3 client. Without it, you must set S3 credentials manually. With it, DuckDB resolves credentials the same way the AWS CLI and boto3 do.
The credential provider order (standard AWS SDK chain):

items.text: EC2 instance metadata service (IMDS) — for EC2/ECS/Lambda execution environments
In a Lambda or ECS container,
picks up the task role automatically — no key management.
In CI, it picks up the environment variables set by GitHub Actions OIDC or similar systems.
so developers with multi-account setups use the right credentials without configuration.

runs.text: Do not hardcode AWS keys in SQL scripts or notebook cells. Use
with the credential chain instead — it is safer and works across all execution environments without modification.
— you delegate credential resolution to the SDK's standard chain rather than managing keys yourself.

cards.title: The Azure Extension
cards.teaser: The Azure extension provides the same credential-chain convenience for Azure Blob Storage and ADLS Gen2 that the AWS extension provides for S3.

runs.text: extension adds native Azure Blob Storage and Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS) access using the Azure SDK credential providers.

items.text: Simple for local development and testing.
On Azure VMs, AKS pods, or Azure Functions, a managed identity is picked up automatically — no stored secrets.
, DuckDB picks up the CLI's cached token via

runs.text: The Azure extension uses the Azure SDK's
under the hood — the same credential resolution order used by the Azure SDK for Python, .NET, and Java. If your application already authenticates via managed identity, DuckDB will too.
Like the AWS extension but for Azure — the SDK's credential chain handles environment, managed identity, and CLI login so you do not wire credentials manually.

cards.title: Community Extensions
cards.teaser: Community extensions extend DuckDB beyond what the core team ships — the extension repository, trust levels, and discovery workflow are worth knowing before you need one.

runs.text: Beyond official extensions, DuckDB has a growing community extension registry. Any developer can publish an extension to the community repository; users install them with an explicit trust acknowledgment.

items.text: Bundled in the DuckDB binary. Fully trusted, signed by the DuckDB team.
, signed by the DuckDB team. Installed with
, signed by the extension author's key (not the DuckDB team). Require
— Hilbert curve space-filling index for spatial locality.
— PRQL language support as an alternative query syntax.

runs.text: Community extensions have not been audited by the DuckDB team. Review the extension source code and trust the author before installing in production environments with access to sensitive data.
Like npm packages vs Node's built-in modules — the registry is rich and community-maintained, but you take on responsibility for vetting what you install.

cards.title: Writing a Custom Extension
cards.teaser: Custom extensions are C++ shared libraries that hook into DuckDB's extension API — the investment pays off when you need a capability unavailable in any existing extension.

runs.text: DuckDB's extension API is a C++ interface that allows you to register custom functions, table functions, file system implementations, and parser extensions. Since DuckDB 1.0 stabilized the C extension ABI, custom extensions can be compiled once and loaded across DuckDB 1.x versions.
When to invest in a custom extension:

items.text: You need to read a proprietary file format natively (e.g., your company's internal binary format).
You want to expose a domain-specific function that DuckDB does not provide (e.g., a custom hash function, a proprietary encoding/decoding operation).
You are building a DuckDB distribution (like MotherDuck) and need to add capabilities at the engine level.
You need a custom virtual file system (e.g., reading from an internal blob store with non-standard auth).

explanation.code:
# Use the DuckDB extension template repository as a starting point
git clone https://github.com/duckdb/extension-template my-extension
cd my-extension && make

# Load the built extension
duckdb -c "LOAD '/path/to/my_extension.duckdb_extension'; SELECT my_hash('hello');"

runs.text: Getting started is easier with the official
repository on GitHub — it handles the CMake setup, CI configuration, and versioning scaffolding.

Before writing a custom extension, check whether a table macro or a Python UDF achieves the same goal. Python UDFs (available via the DuckDB Python API) can call arbitrary Python code and are far easier to write and maintain than C++ extensions for most use cases.

Like writing a native Node.js addon with N-API — you drop down to C++ when the scripting layer cannot express the performance or capability you need, and you use the stable ABI to avoid recompiling on every engine update.
